CASE STUDY
You work as a Customer Care Representative at a mid-size bank in Georgia, Better Bank of Georgia. Your bank is known for its longevity in the banking industry and especially in Georgia. Your bank prides itself on knowing what its customers need and in providing the products and services to match those needs. The bank has recently been running a number of promotions to entice customers with excellent rates on personal loans, mortgages and lines of credit.
You are schooled on all these products and can answer questions and inquiries when your customers call.
You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge). You should “answer the call” with the following greeting:
Better Bank of Georgia, this is _______ (your name). How may I help you?

2015 RLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
CASE STUDY B
JUDGES’ NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION
This is a role-playing event with one judge assuming the role of a Better Bank of Georgia customer.
Review the Judges’ Instructions and the Case Study Situation. One judge will assume the customer role or each judge may vary the problem and ask questions. If all judges ask questions, consider it a family that has quite a few concerns. Remember, what you do for one presentation, must be done for all presentations.
After introductions, you need to explain the reason for the call and ask for some action to resolve the problem.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Time allowed is five minutes.
You will close the event.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS
You have been in the market for a line of credit (LOC) to provide you with a safety net for emergencies in case something unplanned and catastrophic happens in your life that requires immediate funds. You saw ads by the Better Bank of Georgia promoting LOCs, plus the Bank was recommended by a colleague as a friendly and reputable bank with good rates on a line of credit—better rates, you researched, than your major bank was currently offering. So, you started the process of opening the LOC with the Better Bank of Georgia. You were promised closing costs of $272 on a line of $25,000. You thought this was fair, so you proceeded with the line.
Then, in an email, you were informed there had been an error in the calculation and that the closing costs would instead be $832.
You were absolutely floored by the fee difference. You couldn’t believe what you were reading. You feel this is an outrageous amount, not what you were promised, and certainly not what you want to pay.  Being told one amount and then being quoted three times more makes you feel you’re experiencing a “bait and switch” by the bank. At this point, feeling lied to, you’re considering canceling the line and going somewhere else. You’re calling to get the closing costs down to what was promised.

Presentation time is five minutes. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a yellow card indicating one minute is left and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a red card indicating time is up.
The presentation is interactive with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentation. A judge will assume the role of a Prompt Delivery Service (PDS) customer. You play the role of a customer service rep at Prompt Delivery Service (PDS).
Each participant will be given two note cards.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS
Select a logical solution with positive and negative aspects of its implementation
Use appropriate business language and well organized and clearly stated thoughts and statements
Demonstrate ability to effectively communicate with customer
Describe an effective, efficient, and spontaneous action for customer service
Demonstrate efficient, accountable customer service
Exhibit good decision-making and problem-solving skills

CASE STUDY
You work as a Customer Care Representative at a major international delivery company, Prompt Delivery Service (PDS). You are very proud that your company is known for its on-time deliveries worldwide. PDS prides itself on fast response times, meeting deadlines, and making its customers happy.
Customers contact you with questions about their deliveries. You primarily handle customer issues via email, but you also take incoming calls to the service center. You have been trained to track packages and handle most customer complaints.
Your goal is to determine an equitable solution that will be acceptable to both the customer and to PDS.
You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge). You should “answer the call” with the following greeting:
“Prompt Delivery Service, this is ______ (your name). How may I help you?”

2015 SLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
FINAL CASE STUDY
JUDGES’ NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION
This is a role-playing event with one judge assuming the role of a Prompt Delivery Service (PDS) customer.
Review the Judges’ Instructions and the Case Study Situation. One judge will assume the customer role or each judge may vary the problem and ask questions. If all judges ask questions, consider it a family that has quite a few concerns. Remember, what you do for one presentation, must be done for all presentations.
After introductions, you need to explain the reason for the call and ask for some action to resolve the problem.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Time allowed is five minutes.  At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a yellow card indicating one minute is left and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a red card indicating time is up.
You will close the event.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS
This holiday season has been unusually stressful for you because in addition to all the usual hubbub, your daughter announced in September that she, along with her husband and three children, would not be celebrating the holidays with you this year. This would be the first year for the two of you to be apart since she was born. Unexpectedly, she received a two-year assignment to Sydney, Australia. Although you’re sad about not having your only daughter and grandkids with you for the holidays, you’re also excited for your daughter’s career opportunity. But, now, in addition to all the shopping and gift wrapping, you had to arrange for on-time Christmas delivery of the gifts as well.
So, you contacted PDS to find out all the important details about getting packages sent overseas to arrive on time for Christmas. For you, on-time delivery was your overriding concern and that meant delivery by Christmas Eve. You were assured of on-time delivery and no problem, as PDS prides itself on its reputation of promptness. You felt reassured.
You call your daughter to find out if the gifts have arrived and are told no. Christmas Eve comes and goes and no gifts. In desperation, you call to see if the gifts can be delivered Christmas Day. You are told no due to the overwhelming volume and weather issues this year. You are beside yourself. Not only are you without your daughter and your grandkids, your gifts for them are not even there. The gifts finally arrive three days later.
You are furious. You feel betrayed by a company you trusted, and are calling to request reimbursement for what you spent, plus compensation because of your “pain and suffering.”

Select a logical solution with positive and negative aspects of its implementation
Use appropriate business language and well organized and clearly stated thoughts and statements
Demonstrate ability to effectively communicate with customer
Describe an effective, efficient, and spontaneous action for customer service
Demonstrate efficient, accountable customer service
Exhibit good decision-making and problem-solving skills

CASE STUDY
You work as a Customer Care Representative at Greene’s Lawn & Garden Care which has a stellar reputation for troubleshooting and working miracles on lawns and gardens in trouble.
Your customers contact you with all kinds of questions and complaints about their lawns and gardens. Although you have some training and firsthand experience with lawns and gardens, you are certainly not an expert and as a result, need to be careful about diagnosing or recommending particular solutions.
You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge). You should “answer the call” with the following greeting:
“Greene’s Lawn & Garden Care, this is _____ (your name). How may I help you?”

2015 SLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
PRELIMINARY CASE STUDY
JUDGES’ NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION
This is a role-playing event with one judge assuming the role of a Green’s Lawn & Garden Care customer.
Review the Judges’ Instructions and the Case Study Situation. One judge will assume the customer role or each judge may vary the problem and ask questions. If all judges ask questions, consider it a family that has quite a few concerns. Remember, what you do for one presentation, must be done for all presentations.
After introductions, you need to explain the reason for the call and ask for some action to resolve the problem.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Time allowed is five minutes.
You will close the event.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS
You are extremely unhappy with Greene’s and very disappointed because of their stellar reputation. You expected better results. You are very proud of your lawn and have kept it in excellent condition over the years with Greene’s help. So, you thought they would be “Johnny on the spot” with your lawn’s problem. Unfortunately, that has not been the case for you. And you’re wondering if, as a long-time customer, you’re being taken for granted.
This past summer with all the rain, you noticed something very wrong in your lawn. You called Greene’s and asked if someone would come out and take a look. They said they would and you set a date. The day came and went and no Greene’s. You called a second time to report the no show and got an effusive apology and a second date for a diagnosis.
A technician came, although later than the time frame promised. He diagnosed the problem as bugs and sprayed. It didn’t work, and got worse. You called a third time to report the problem and were told the supervisor would come out that day and take a look. The supervisor came, but it was the next day. He diagnosed it as a fungus, and applied a chemical treatment. But to no avail.
You are beside yourself with frustration. First, with the problem, and then, with Greene’s who you felt had your back and now has let you down. You’re petrified that this fungus is going to overtake your beautiful lawn. You want the problem fixed and at their expense.

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS
You have 10 minutes to review the case.
Presentation time is five minutes. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating one minute is left and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
You work as a Customer Care Representative at a well-known pest control company in the Southeast called Best Pest Management.
Each team member will be given two note cards. Note cards will be collected following the presentation.
All team members must participate in the presentation as well as answer the questions posed by the judges. Cover all the points described in the case.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS
Logical solution is selected and presented with positive and negative aspects of its implementation given
Thoughts and statements are well organized and clearly stated; appropriate business language is used
Ability to effectively communicate with customer
Explain an effective, efficient, and spontaneous action for customer service
Empower yourself to give efficient, accountable customer service
Exhibit good decision-making and problem-solving skills

CASE STUDY SITUATION
You work as a Customer Care Representative at a well-known pest control company in the Southeast called Best Pest Management.  Best provides homeowners and businesses with comprehensive pest control services and is known for its fair prices and responsive customer care.
Customers contact you with all kinds of bug and rodent issues.   Your job is to sort through the concern and direct the individual to the department that can help.  You also field customer complaints, so every day is challenging.  You never quite know what you’ll be faced with.
Your goal with this call is to determine an equitable solution that will be acceptable to the customer and to Best Pest Management.
You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge).  You should “answer the call” with the following greeting:
“Best Pest Management, this is _______ (your name).  How may I help you?”

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION
This is a role-playing event with the judges serving as managers for Best Pest Management.
Review the Judges’ Instructions and the Case Study Situation. After the introductions, you should begin the phone conversation by calling Best Pest Management.  In this performance, the judge will assume the role of a Best Pest Management customer.
The team members will then begin their dialogue with the owners (judges).
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Each of the members of the team should respond to at least one question or issue. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
You will close the event by thanking the participants for their input and recommendations. The participants will hand in their note cards before leaving.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS
In this performance, the judge will assume the role of a Best Pest Management customer.
You hired Best because you had a major spider problem and heard by word of mouth the company has a good track record solving problems quickly.  You explained about the spider problem and were told a technician would be sent to your home.  The technician who came, sprayed, and said the treatment would solve the problem and the spiders would be gone after two weeks.  It did not happen.  The spiders are still there and are a major nuisance.  You were also told by the technician that if the spiders weren’t gone after two weeks, they would spray again for free.
When you called to tell Best you still had spiders, and requested the additional treatment, the man who claimed he was in charge said they would not spray again for free, rather, it would be at an additional cost.  When you told “the man in charge” of his technician’s promise to come and spray again for free, he said he didn’t care.  You were dumfounded at his response!  You want a full refund and told “the man in charge” you would report this to the Better Business Bureau.
There is no right or wrong answer to the event. However, in a team event, the ability of the team members to work together to come to a consensus, based on the facts provided (and those that you care to add) should be the basis for the score. The individual or team should present its suggestions with clarity and conviction.

Feel free to enhance the story as much as you want, but if you enhance it for one, make sure you enhance it for all the groups.

The team members will then begin their dialogue with the owners (judges).
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Each of the members of the team should respond to at least one question or issue. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and at five minutes he timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
You will close the event by thanking the participants for their input and recommendations. The participants will hand in their note cards before leaving.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS
In this performance, the judge will assume the role of a Dependable Interiors’ customer.
You hired Dependable Interiors to install two bathroom sinks and a full wall mirror.  You selected Dependable because of their reputation.  Your expectation was it would be fast and easy with their years of experience.
You paid the company $399 and the work was supposed to be completed with two men over eight hours.  Dependable sent one worker for four hours.  They canceled some appointments and you weren’t available for other times they proposed because you were out of town.  The company later offered to send four men for eight hours, but didn’t follow through.  You’re thinking that because the job was so small, Dependable didn’t give it any priority.  The work is incomplete, you’re questioning the quality of the work, and you are not happy.  You want a $200 refund for the hours of work you didn’t receive and the remainder of the work finished to your satisfaction.

2012 NLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
FINAL CASE STUDY

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS

You have 10 minutes to review the case.
Presentation time is five minutes. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a card indicating one minute is left and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
The presentation is interactive with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentation. A judge will assume the role of a JC Dollar department store customer.
You will play the role of the customer care associate in the call center.
Each participant will be given two note cards.
Be prepared to answer questions posed by the judges.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS

Logical solution is selected and presented with positive and negative aspects of its implementation given
Thoughts and statements are well organized and clearly stated; appropriate business language is used
Ability to effectively communicate with customer
Explain an effective, efficient, and spontaneous action for customer service
Empower yourself to give efficient, accountable customer service
Exhibits good decision-making and problem-solving skills

CASE STUDY SITUATION
You work for JC Dollar department store as a Customer Care Associate in the call center. You work exclusively with customers who have charge accounts with the chain. JC Dollar is one of the largest department stores in the Midwest.

JC Dollar is a typical department store with Men’s, Women’s, and Children’s Clothing, Shoes, Home Goods, and Household Appliances.

You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge). When customers call, you request their account number to locate their computer record and view their purchases, payments, and returns history. You should “answer the call” with the following greeting:

“Good afternoon, this is _____ (your name). How may I help you?”

2012 NLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
FINAL CASE STUDY
JUDGES’ NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION
This is a role-playing event with one judge assuming the role of a JC Dollar department store customer.
Review the Judges’ Instructions and the Case Study Situation. One judge will assume the customer role or each judge may vary the problem and ask questions. If all judges ask questions, consider it a family that quite a few concerns. Remember, what you do for one presentation, must be done for all presentations.
After introductions, you need to explain the reason for the call and ask for some action to resolve the problem.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Time allowed is five minutes.
You will close the event.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS

In this performance, the judge will assume the role of a JC Dollar department store customer.

You purchased a vacuum cleaner two months ago and chose the least-expensive model. The vacuum cleaner came with a 30-day all-inclusive warranty. You did not purchase the extended warranty because you have not had any problems with your previous vacuum cleaners and they all worked fine for many years.

You call the JC Dollar customer service number and reach a Customer Service Representative.  You explain that a few days after the 30-day warranty expired, you used the vacuum to clean your dog’s bed. Now the vacuum no longer suctions correctly.

You want the store to replace your defective, less-expensive model with the more-powerful, more-expensive model, since your model was of such poor quality.

2012 NLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
PRELIMINARY CASE STUDY

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS
You have 10 minutes to review the case.
Presentation time is five minutes. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a card indicating one minute left and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
The presentation is interactive with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentation. A judge will assume the role of a Computers R Us customer. You will play the role of the customer care representative at Computers R Us.
Each participant will be given two note cards.
Be prepared to answer questions posed by the judges.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS
Logical solution is selected and presented with positive and negative aspects of its implementation given
Thoughts and statements are well organized and clearly stated; appropriate business language is used
Ability to effectively communicate with customer
Explain an effective, efficient, and spontaneous action for customer service
Empower yourself to give efficient, accountable customer service
Exhibits good decision-making and problem-solving skills

CASE STUDY SITUATION
You work as a Customer Care Representative at a major national computer store, Computers R Us. Your company is known for its great prices as well as superior service. Your company prides itself on fast response times and making the customer happy.
Customers contact you with questions and complaints about everything from delivery concerns, installing software, how to fix computer problems and general complaints about the store, staff, and service.
You are authorized to offer customers up to $100 if it will resolve the issue and satisfy the customer. Your challenge is to determine an equitable solution that will be acceptable to the customer and to your company.
You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge). You should “answer the call” with the following greeting:
“Computers R Us, this is _____ (your name). How may I help you?”

2012 NLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
PRELIMINARY CASE STUDY
JUDGES’ NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION
This is a role-playing event with one judge assuming the role of a Computers R Us customer.
Review the Judges’ Instructions and the Case Study Situation. One judge will assume the customer role or each judge may vary the problem and ask questions. If all judges ask questions, consider it a family that quite a few concerns. Remember, what you do for one presentation, must be done for all presentations.
After introductions, you need to explain the reason for the call and ask for some action to resolve the problem.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Time allowed is five minutes.
You will close the event.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS

On Saturday, you bought a monitor, hard drive, and printer from Computers R Us. They were running a special promotion which guaranteed two hours of free technical support in your home, in the store, or on the phone to set up your new hardware.

When you called from home the next day (Sunday) in order to use the technical support promised, you were told to call back in an hour, since all of the support technicians were busy.

You called back an hour later and they again asked you to call back in an hour.

On your third call, you asked if you could set up an appointment for any time today up until 10 p.m. in order to get your new hardware installed prior to going back to work on Monday. They said they do not take appointments; that the technicians are very busy because of the special offer, and to call back in an hour.

You made the purchases because of the offer of free technical support and you want the technical support today so that you do not have to take off from work tomorrow.

If they cannot help you today, you want some type of compensation from them in lieu of the technical support they offered, but are not providing.

Once again, you call the Computers R Us store.



# Client Service Case Study - 2009 RLC

- Category: Client Service
- Source file: `Client Service/Client Service Case Study - 2009 RLC.docx`
- Extracted words: 775

---

FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
2009 Regional Problem

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS

You have 10 minutes to review the scenario and may use the provided note cards in the presentation.
You will have an interactive 5-minute presentation with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentation.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS
Communications skills—the ability to exchange information and ideas with others through writing, speaking, reading, or listening
Analytical skills—the ability to derive facts from data, findings from facts, conclusions from findings, and recommendations from conclusions
Production skills—the ability to take a concept from an idea and make it real
Teamwork—the ability to be an effective member of a productive group
Priorities/time management—the ability to determine priorities and manage time commitments

CASE STUDY SITUATION

The Samuel family has reserved a room at Hotel Bluebird in Chestertown, PA, where you are a client service representative.  They have reserved the room for January 10, 2009.

The Samuel family booked the room 9 (nine) months ago when they were planning their vacation trip to Florida.  During their travels, they will be traveling through Chestertown visiting family 5 hours away.

Unknown to the Samuel family, this night is the Monster Truck Rally, and every hotel in town has been booked for months.

The Samuel family confirmed the reservation earlier in the week and made sure that the room was guaranteed for late arrival.  They plan on arriving after 12 midnight due to their travel plans.

On the morning of January 10, 2009, the computers crash at Hotel Bluebird, and all hotel reservations are lost.  The Reservations Department had a hard copy from earlier in the week, so they feel that they have everything under control.  Client service representatives check in the customers throughout the day and evening with no problems/issues.

The Samuel family arrives in town at 12:30 a.m.  The family goes to check in, and Hotel Bluebird doesn’t have a room reserved for them.  The hotel is completely full with no rooms vacant.

Your job as the only client service representative on staff for the evening is to try and accommodate the Samuel family.  Propose your recommendations to the Samuel family.

FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
JUDGE’S INSTRUCTIONS
2009 Regional Problem

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION

In preparation for this event, you should review the following information with all judges:
The judges play an interactive role in this event.
A time keeper will stand at 6 minutes and 7 minutes.
Participants may conduct a slightly different approach and/or discussion with you each time; however, it is important that the information you provide and the questions you ask be uniform for every participant.
Please use a critical and consistent eye in rating each participant

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS

CASE STUDY SITUATION

The Samuel family has reserved a room at Hotel Bluebird in Chestertown, PA, where you are a client service representative.  They have reserved the room for January 10, 2009.

The Samuel family booked the room 9 (nine) months ago when they were planning their vacation trip to Florida.  During there travels, they will be traveling through Chestertown visiting family 5 hours away.

Unknown to the Samuel family, this night is the Monster Truck Rally and every hotel in town has been booked for months.

The Samuel family confirmed the reservation earlier in the week and made sure that the room was guaranteed for late arrival.  They plan on arriving after 12 midnight due to their travel plans.

On the morning of January 10, 2009, the computers crash at Hotel Bluebird and all hotel reservations are lost.  The Reservations Department had a hard copy from earlier in the week, so they feel that they have everything under control.  Client service representatives check in the customers throughout the day and evening.

The Samuel family arrives in town at 12:30 a.m.  The family goes to check in and Hotel Bluebird doesn’t have a room reserved for them.

Your job as the only client service representative on staff for the evening is to try and accommodate the Samuel family.  Propose your recommendations to the Samuel family.

During the course of the role-play you are to ask the following questions of each participant:
How do you explain the error to the customer?
Will you call your manager or will you try to handle the situation on your own?
What are several recommendations/solutions you will give to the problem?
What is the most important thing that the customer will remember when they leave the Hotel?

Once the consultant (participant) has made his/her presentation and has answered your questions, you will conclude the role-play by saying you will consider his/her recommendations.
You are not to make any comments after the event is over, except to thank the participant



# Client Service_2008 RLC

PENNSYLVANIA FUTURE BUSINESS LEADERS OF AMERICA
2008 REGION LEADERSHIP CONFERENCE

CLIENT SERVICE

Complete the following information on the answer sheet.
PRINT your NAME on the first line following the word NAME.
On the SUBJECT line, print the name of the EVENT.
Following the words TEST NO., print the test number as found on the front of your test.
On the DATE line, print the name of your SCHOOL.
Following the word HOUR, print the number of your REGION.

DO NOT OPEN THE TEST UNTIL DIRECTED TO DO SO.

All answers must be recorded on the answer sheet.

Read each question carefully before answering.  When you have decided which answer is most correct, find the space on the answer sheet following the number of the problem, and with a PENCIL blacken the entire space in the proper column.  Do not make any other pencil marks on the answer sheet, as the scoring machine will reject it.

Be sure you have a # 2 pencil with an eraser.  DO NOT use a ballpoint pen.

If you are given TRUE-FALSE questions, please mark A for TRUE and B for FALSE in the space on the answer sheet following the number of the problem.  For MULTIPLE CHOICE questions, mark the appropriate letter in the space on the answer sheet following the number of the problem.

No other equipment or materials are permitted to complete the test.

ACCURACY IS OF UTMOST IMPORTANCE!  In the case of a tie, the competitor who progresses farthest through the test without making any errors shall be rated higher.  However, if there should be no errors or in case the participants shall have identical errors, the person submitting his or her test to the event administrator first, will be rated higher.

You are allowed 60 minutes for writing the test.  You will be given a starting signal and a warning after 55 minutes have elapsed.

If you should complete the test before the allotted time, take all papers to the designated person who will mark the order in which the tests are submitted.  This time factor, however, shall be used only in the event a second tie breaker is needed.

Mark the correct answer on your Scantron sheet for each of the following questions.

Only extremely satisfied customers are genuinely loyal.
True
False

You can’t truly become customer-centric unless the entire organization is integrated so that everyone touches the customers.
True
False

Follow-up is one of the aspects of effective customer service.
True.
False.

An effective salesperson does not take the time to get to know their clients.
True.
False.

A salesperson must be willing to think outside the box and not always stick standard practice.
True.
False.

Salespeople should offer services common in their other industries but not necessarily common in their industry.
True.
False.

There is no one way to hug; it can be a smile, a high-five, expedited services, or sending out three-cent stamps.
True.
False.

One of the Golden Principles of business is to start out “everyone on the floor.”
True.
False.

The owner’s personality becomes the business.
True.
False.

Bad times hit all business, and when they do the salesperson can cut back things that the customer will notice.
True.
False.

The order of importance in client-service is your product, your service, and then your employees.
True.
False.

If client-service associates don’t build positive and personal relationships with one another, the organization can become dysfunctional.
True.
False.

Satisfied customers tell more people about their experiences than unsatisfied customers.
True.
False.

Technology is the backbone of any great company and it should be designed to help the customers understand the needs of the business.
True.
False.

Customer service only occurs after a problem arises.
True.
False.

Most technology systems focus on inventory or financial issues and often ignore the client base.
True.
False.

Most businesses gather data and always know how to use it effectively to meet the customer’s needs.
True.
False.

In order to win big clients, you need to plan, prepare, and practice.
True.
False.

Use entertainment as a means to further develop your business relationship with a client.
True.
False.

When you answer a call, and the customer really needs to resolve the issue with another department, it is your responsibility to make sure the customer reaches someone who can help.
True.
False.

If you want to have the best product, always have the vendors or suppliers come to you.
True.
False.

Customers always remember when you make a mistake; but they never remember how you fixed or corrected the mistake.
True.
False.

If you are traveling domestically with a client, always sit in economy class.
True.
False.

In business e-mails, it is appropriate to use abbreviations such as lol or ttyl?
True.
False.

If you are going on vacation give your client your hotel number and a fax number.
True.
False.

You can delegate authority but you can not delegate responsibility.
True.
False.

Start every client meeting by stating the purpose of the meeting and never discuss how long the meeting will last.
True.
False.

Customer responsiveness involves all the contact a customer has with your company from the point at which he or she is a prospective customer until the point the sale is completed.
True.
False.

The client-service associate should never be afraid of leveling with, or coaching a client.
True.
False.

It is acceptable to ask your client to identify who they would rate as their best business partner.
True.
False.

Customer-centric means:
What the salesperson wants always comes first.
The price of a product leads all decisions.
The customer is reactive versus proactive.
The customer is the center of the universe.

Hugging with regard to client-service is defined as:
A mind-set.
Touchy-feely type of sales promotion.
Physical acts of compassion.
Greeting a client the first time you meet them.

If you want customers to stay, then you have to assure them:
They have made the right decision in choosing to do business with you.
You are going to be there to assist them if they have any difficulty.
You are going to hold your organization and its people accountable to ensure that the job gets done.
All of the above.

The central area where the checkout is, is called the:
Living room.
Kitchen.
Bathroom.
Garage.

Salespeople gather information on their clients by:
Listening and learning during the selling process.
Completing customer surveys.
Calling other individuals within the company to find out information about the client.
All of the above.

Your business should feel like a home – customers should be:
Addressed by first names.
Served coffee and snacks.
Offered diversions for their children.
All of the above.

The Three E’s of client-service are:
Energy, Excellence, Eagerness
Eagerness, Execution, Embracement
Enthusiasm, Execution, Excellence
Energy, Enthusiasm, Execution

Climbing the pyramid means:
Focusing on the customers that buy the most.
Never forgetting the customers on the bottom.
Both a and b are correct statements.
Neither a nor b are correct statements.

To further a client-service relationship, use your technology to:
Send a standard e-mail to all customers.
Write personalized letters to your customers.
Leave a voice mail message for your customers.
Don’t make any additional contact with the customer.

A client-service associate should be:
Committed to the community.
Not involved in community activities.
Participate in one community activity per year.
Avoid all contact within the community they work.

In order to avoid mistakes, a client-service associate should:
Track mistakes corporate-wide so they don’t happen more than once.
Track mistakes and share them with only upper-management.
Track mistakes and share them with only the client-service department.
Don’t track mistakes at all.

An innovative way to develop new ideas is:
Form a group within your company.
Form a group with other non-competing businesses in your field.
Form a group with upper-management.
Form a group with other competing businesses in your field.

A customer who complains usually wants to:
Get the product for free.
Call attention to him or her.
Have his or her experience validated.
Contact the Better Business Bureau.

The client wants the product shipped on Monday.  You are not able to ship the product until Wednesday.  Which of the following statements is the best way to tell your customer?
I can’t ship your product on Monday.
I can’t ship your product until Wednesday.
I can ship your product on Wednesday.
I understand that you would like the product shipped on Monday.  Due to our manufacturing schedule, we can ship it to you first thing Wednesday morning.

A car manufacturer faced a problem when the rear door locks were found to be faulty on its minivans.  Those who were loyal customers found:
Their perceptions about all other aspects of the minivan were impacted.
They questioned whether other features of the car were reliable.
They felt all locks would eventually be found to be faulty.
They understood the lock was faulty and the problem would be corrected.

Which of the following are qualities customers look for in a client-service professional?
Courtesy, integrity, knowledge of products and services, reliability
Courtesy, honesty, knowledge of products and services, reliability
Courtesy, decision making ability, honesty, reliability
Courtesy, decision making ability, reliability, knowledge of products and services

You are speaking with a customer who is upset with your company.  He sat home all morning waiting for a delivery that did not arrive.  There is no way you can undo what already happened.  What you say next can either calm the customer or upset him further.  Which of the following is the best response to this customer?
“Mr. Johnson, there is nothing I can do.”
“Mr. Johnson, I am sorry for the mistake.  Let me check right now to see what I can do for you.”
“Mr. Johnson, let me check into this.  I’ll get back to you tomorrow.”
“Mr. Johnson, the only thing I can do is offer you free shipping on your next order.”

Which of the following is not acceptable when communicating with a customer?
Interrupting the customer while they are speaking.
Remaining objective and not jumping to conclusions.
Paying attention to non-verbal communication.
Refraining from becoming emotional.

Customer service should be based on:
What you think the customer wants.
What upper-management thinks the customer wants.
What the customer actually wants.
All of the above are ways to measure customer service.

Which of the following is an example of objective customer service measurement?
Sales figures
Interpersonal relations
Oral and written communication
Customer satisfaction results.

Which of the following is an example of subjective customer service measurement?
Sales figures
Interpersonal relations
Productivity
Expenses

Mrs. Jones just pulled into your parking lot.  She is a customer who complains about everything.  Your coworkers start making jokes about her and tell you that you will need to handle her.  Your response is:
Make your own jokes to your co-workers.
Ignore Mrs. Jones and let another client-service professional handle her.
Help Mrs. Jones as quickly as possible.
Provide exceptional client-service to Mrs. Jones.

In which of the following situations is client-service follow-up necessary?
When a customer is upset with your company.
When a customer has unrealistic expectations.
When a customer completely understood what they were purchasing.
None of the above situations warrant follow-up contact.

While talking with a customer that is upset, you know that you need to contact the customer in a few days to make sure everything is ok.  Which of the following statements is the best way to let the customer know you will be contacting them again?
I’ll call you back after I research this issue.
I’ll get back to you as soon as possible.
I’ll call you on Friday with an answer.
All of the above statements are acceptable.

In order to be an exceptional client-service associate, it is important for you to:
Learn all you can about your products and services.
Learn all you can about your competitors products and services.
Learn about your entire company operation.
All of the above are important to being an exceptional client-service associate.
.
Which of the following is the most important aspect of any job?
Meeting deadlines set by your manager.
Developing new products.
Customer service.
Being a reliable employee.

You are working with a client from another country.  Their customs are different than your customs.  Which of the following statements best describes how you should handle this situation?
Don’t’ worry about the differences in customs.  All foreigners are aware of customs in the United States and will adapt to your customs.
Learn all you can about the clients customs.  This will show the client that you are genuinely interested in them.
Allow your client to demonstrate their customs, but don’t spend much time learning about them; focus on the business at hand.
Customs don’t matter in business.

You are employed at Pencils-R-Us.  A customer contacts you on Tuesday with an error in her order.  She received the 500 pencils that were supposed to say “East Fox Elementary School” and was ordered in blue and gold.  They arrived as red and white with “East Font Elementary School.”  The pencils are to be used for each elementary student on Monday fro a special program.  They paid an additional $50 for a rush order.  Which of the following statements is the best way to meet the needs of the client?
We absolutely can’t fill this order for you on Monday.
We can’t guarantee you will have the order on Monday.
We can refund you 50% of the purchase price with the understanding that you won’t receive the order until Tuesday.
We can place a rush on the correct order and pay for overnight shipping with 100% refund if it does not arrive on Monday.

Use the scenario in question 49 to answer the following question.  Which of the following dates is the first time to follow-up with the client?
Friday afternoon.
Monday morning.
Monday afternoon.
Tuesday morning.

If your store does not offer the particular product or service the customer is looking for, your first option should always be to:
Convince the customer he doesn’t need it anyway.
Suggest alternatives that your store does carry.
Immediately refer him to a competitor.
Inform him of the drawbacks of the product he is seeking.

When dealing with client-service issues, clients most want:
The human touch.
Automated technology (e-mail, voice mail, etc.)
Limited contact with the client-service professional.
None of the above.

Clients are interested in:
Reliability of your people.
Reliability of your product.
Reliability of your service and customer experience.
All of the above.

When speaking with a client on the telephone, which of the following should be avoided?
Smiling.
Chewing gum.
A quiet atmosphere.
Speaking with a soothing voice.

Today, client presentations are usually delivered with the help of PowerPoint slides.  Which of the following is a common mistake in these presentations?
Keeping is short and precise.
Keeping slides clear and uncluttered.
Knowing your main points and covering them multiple times.
Including every point of the presentation on the slides.

Often it takes days or weeks to answer a question that a client may pose.  In the interim, what is the best way to keep the client informed?
E-mail the client on a regular basis.
Don’t contact the client until you find out the answer.
Call the client and say “I have not forgotten about your question and we should have an answer soon.”
Send a letter to the client informing them that you haven’t forgotten about their question and that you should have an answer soon.

Which of the following types of listening involves hearing the spoken message as well as the unspoken clues in the words, tones, and pacing?
Passive
Selective
Active
Inactive

In today’s age with communications, client-service associates should provide clients with the following:
Your cell phone number.
Your home phone number.
Your e-mail address.
All of the above.

Which of the following statements would be most appropriate to use with a client?
Let’s review what we know.
Can we get back on task?
Why are you really here?
Let’s get to the point of this call.

If something goes wrong with a sale under your supervision, you should:
Blame the manufacturing department.
Blame the co-worker who assisted you with the sale.
Tell the customer he filled out the paperwork incorrectly.
Take responsibility for the mistake and don’t make excuses.

Which of the following is not a good way to create long-term customer relationships?
Using a gimmick to attract new customers.
Creating a positive corporate image.
Engaging in appropriate business actions.
All of the above.

When solving a customers problem, which of the following should be done first?
Generate options.
Analyze the situation.
Implement the solution.
Evaluate options.

Which of the following is the appropriate way to end a telephone conversation with a client?
Let the client hang up first.
Let the client-service associate hang up first.
Thank the client for calling and then hang up.
It doesn’t matter how a telephone conversation is ended.

Disconnecting from a customer may happen by:
Providing client-service follow-up at regular intervals.
Providing products that meet customer’s needs.
Offering the same types of promotions.
None of the above causes customer disconnection.

In all industries, clients want things fast, fast, fast.  We must empower clients with self-sufficiency.  Examples of self-sufficiency may include:
Automated teller machines.
Self check-in at airports.
None of the above.
Both a and b.

Your current customers have the following concerns about new customers you may acquire:
Additional customers mean you can provide a lower price.
Additional customers mean you will have less time for existing customers.
Additional customers mean growth for your business and too much growth you may forget about existing customers.
All of the above are concerns.

If a project needs to get done for a client and there is no one at the right level to do it:
Contact the correct department personally and make sure the project is finished.
Roll up your sleeves and tackle it yourself.
Contact your assistant and make sure he or she completes the project.
Tell your client it can’t be done.

Merchandising the client-service associates success is not done by:
Grandstanding, bragging, and showing off.
Letting the client know your past successes.
Not grandstanding, bragging, and showing off.
Keeping the client apprised of what you are up to.

A probing question:
Usually elicits a “yes” or “no” answer.
Requests information in a way that requires more of a complete answer than a simply “yes” or “no.”
Helps speed up interactions with people who find it difficult to make a final decision.
Uses information already established to clarify points and ask for more details.

Which of the following is not a tip to consider before answering client questions:
Answer a question with a question.
Decide whether you know the answer.
Smile
Be careful with your power.

Positive language displays all of these questions except:
It does not stress positive actions that would be appropriate.
It tells the client what can be done.
It suggests alternatives and choices available to the client.
It stresses positive actions and consequences that can be anticipated.

When writing to clients, all are important books to have on hand except:
Dictionary
Business magazine
Thesaurus
Office handbook

Active listening is:
Seeking to understand the other person’s position without getting emotionally involved.
Hearing without sending any feedback.
Hearing only what you want to hear.
Listening with your whole mind and body—not just your ears.

What is not a strategy that native speakers can do to become better listeners while non-native clients are speaking?
Pretend to understand what they are saying.
Avoid making judgment about incorrectly accented speech.
Don’t correct grammar and pronunciation errors.
Be a patient listener.

When deciding whether a dress code is appropriate and what it should be, most organizations take into consideration:
The business’s public image.
The nature of the work performed by the employees affected by the dress code.
Safety standards.
All of the above.

Soft skills can be described as:
Punctuality.
Cooperation.
Both A & B.
None of the above.

When a client calls and you must put them on hold, follow all of these basic telephone courtesies except:
Tell the caller why you would like to put him/her on hold.
Ask permission to put them on hold.
Keep callers on hold no longer than 3 minutes.
Offer to call the customer back instead of putting him/her on hold.

When leaving a voicemail message for a client:
State your name, company name, why you are calling, and call back number.
Don’t plan ahead of time what you will say.
Repeat the call back number slowly three times.
State your name, the date and time, your company name, why you are calling, and the call back number.

The National Do Not Call Registry:
Began in July 2007 and requires client service associates to drop from their call lists, the phone numbers of clients who are registered.
Was created to give customers the choice of whether or not to allow telemarketers calls to their home.
Has a registration fee and can be requested on-line or by calling a toll-free number.
Is not enforced by state governments.

When using a business e-mail system, client service associates should bear in mind:
That occasionally sending personal e-mail is okay.
Using all capital letters in e-mails is okay.
The employee owns the e-mail and that the employer doesn’t have the right to review the e-mails.
Each client who sends an e-mail message should receive an immediate reply with an instant reply message.

A client service associate who has an amiable personality type:
Wants to build relationships, loves to give others support and attention, values suggestions from others, and fears disagreements.
Wants to save time, value results, and love being in control and doing things his/her way.
Values appreciation and a pat on the back, loves social situations and parties, likes to inspire others, and is extroverted, readily showing emotion to others.
None of the above.

As a client service associate, it is important to understand generational differences when dealing with clients.  This generation group “works to live” and expects diversity as a fact of life and fully accepts women in positions of power.
Millennials.
Matures.
Baby Boomers.
Generation X.

This generation puts in long hours because of how closely they associate their occupation with their identities.  This generation is the first credit card generation.  In addition, this generation currently makes up the majority of the political, cultural, and academic leadership roles in the United States.
Millennials.
Matures.
Baby Boomers.
Generation X.

Some clients may have a service animal to assist with a disability.  As a client service associate, all of the rules apply except:
The animal is a pet so it is okay to pet the service animal.
Don’t feed the service animal.
Don’t make noises at the animal.
Only assist the client if given permission.

Clients categorize a “value turnoff” by the following example:
Voicemail menus that are unnecessarily complicated.
Slow service and lack of delivery options.
Poor product selection and inadequate reordering processes.
A failure to meet quality expectations and inadequate guarantees.

Clients categorize a “system turnoff” by the following example:
Client service associates projecting an indifferent attitude.
A failure to meet quality expectations and inadequate guarantees.
Unnecessary or repetitious paperwork requirements and a lack of delivery options.
High prices relative to the perceived value of the product or service.

When your boss is not available, you answer the telephone.  What should you say when she is on a coffee break?
I am sorry but she is on a coffee break, Could I please have your name and number and I will get her to call you back when she returns.
I am sorry.  She is right down the hall in the coffee room.  Could I please have your name and number and I will get her to call you back when she returns.
I am sorry, but she can’t come to the phone right now.  Could I please have your name and number and I will get her to call you back when she returns.
You just missed her.  I think she went on her coffee break.  Could I please have your name and number and I will get her to call you back when she returns.

You work for First City Bank.  When calls come in, they go directly to your phone; there is no receptionist.  How should you answer each call?
Thank you for calling First City Bank, how can I help you?
Thank you for calling First City Bank, Crystal speaking, how many I help you?
Crystal speaking, how may I help you?
Thank you for calling First City Bank, Crystal speaking.

While eating lunch at the food court in the mall, Kina, a client service associate at a retail store within the mall, witnesses a customer service incident in action.  A woman approached the counter with a crushed Styrofoam cup and said, “This cup fell off our table and broke.  I need another drink and I need someone to come clean up our table and the floor.”  The tone of her voice suggested that somehow the restaurant was responsible for her broken cup.  What should be done?
Tell the customer that you are very sorry but the cup can’t be replaced because she was the one who accidentally dropped the cup.
Tell the customer that you saw that the cup didn’t drop off the counter, but you could give her a glass of water.  You also volunteer to give her a free French fry coupon.
Tell the customer that you are sorry that this occurred and you will be happy to replace the drink.  Ask her to move to another table because no one can clean up the mess immediately.
Tell the customer that you are sorry that this occurred and you will be happy to replace the drink.  And another employee will be out to clean up the mess immediately.

Macy recently went to work for ABC Company as the receptionist and client service representative.  In the first week, several other employees went out of their way to go through the lobby just to see her.  She is very attractive, and everyone soon learned she was a former local model.  Macy’s image started to create problems within the company.  Though Macy was a nice person and didn’t appear conceited, her appearance was a distraction to the organization.  The office manager discovered that work had slowed own since Macy was hired.  For instance, male sales reps were stopping by and spending time chatting with her; female workers were saying catty things behind her back and seemed to be spending more time having negative conversations.  Three comments overheard were, “She’s too perfect,” “She wears heavy makeup,” and She dresses too nice for this place.”  What would you do if you were the manager of the client service department?
Speak to all employees about the importance of the daily work and that you are having a difficult time understanding why production seems to be lacking.  Discuss with employees about the importance of time management and working as a team.
You feel badly, but unfortunately, Macy is too distracting and you are going to have to let her go from the company.
Speak with Macy about her appearance to see if she can tone down her attire and be careful about her behavior with other employees.
Speak with the male members of the department about appropriate behavior and sexual harassment in the workplace.  Speak with female members of the department about team work and making sure that they meet their production amounts.

You are working in the hotel industry, and an international traveler is asking directions to a local landmark.  Although you have repeated the instructions several times, the traveler just doesn’t seem to understand your directions.  What should you do?
Try and explain the directions one more time.
You know that the person in the Purchasing Department speaks the language of the international traveler but she is out to lunch for 15 more minutes.  Ask the traveler to wait for the 15 minutes and someone will assist him.
Get a map with detailed landmarks and map out the course that the traveler needs to take.
Ask another member in your department to assist you because the other person has great use of body language and she may be able to assist with the traveler.

2010 SLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
FINAL CASE STUDY

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS

You have 10 minutes to review the case.
Presentation time is five minutes. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
The presentation is interactive with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentations. The judges will play the role of the customer. You work for Tri-City Heating in customer service.
Each participant will be given two note cards.
Cover all the points described in the case and be prepared to answer questions posed by the judges.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS

Logical solution is selected and presented with positive and negative aspects of its implementation given
Alternatives are recognized with pros and cons stated and evaluated
Case translated into effective, efficient, and spontaneous action
Exhibits good decision-making and problem-solving skills
Thoughts and statements are well organized and clearly stated; appropriate business language is used
Demonstrates ability to effectively communicate with client

CASE STUDY SITUATION

Your company, Tri-City Heating, has an excellent reputation in the community. Professional, fair, and prompt are some of the company’s strengths. You have a very clear pricing structure, which helps you stand out from the competition.

You have a membership club called the “VIC Club.” This club allows a customer to pay a monthly fee in return for priority service, free annual furnace check-ups, and price reductions. The benefit to your company is that you have a returning customer.

For those customers who are not part of the VIC Club, there is an $85 service call fee, full-price for parts and labor, and you try to get them scheduled as soon as possible, but not as a priority over the VIC members. Your manager wants you to try to sell the VIC membership to as many customers as possible.

You will be fielding a call from a customer (judge). After introductions, you should “answer the call” with the following greeting:

“Tri-City Heating, this is _____ (your name). How may I help you?”

Your role is the customer rep and address/speak to points listed above, as the conversation unfolds.

2010 SLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
FINAL CASE STUDY
JUDGES’ NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION

This is a role-playing event with you acting as a customer of Tri-City Heating.
Review the “Judges’ Instructions” and the “Case Study Situation.”
After introductions, the participant will “take your call.”
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. The time allowed is five minutes. At four minutes the timekeeper will stand and at five minutes the timekeeper will stand and hold up a colored card indicating time is up.
You will close the event by closing the phone conversation.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES’ INSTRUCTIONS

In this performance, the judge will assume the role of a Tri-City Heating VIC customer.
Here is your story: You had your annual furnace check two weeks ago and everything checked out fine. But now, the furnace doesn’t perform as it should nor does it maintain the temperature in the house. The first time the furnace comes on, everything works fine. However, after the first cycle, the furnace only buzzes, as if it is trying to come on, but it doesn’t kick on. The service call sheet that the technician left behind last time said that the furnace turned on, but that was probably the first time only.

Because of the recent visit, you believe that you should not have to pay for another service call (your annual check was “free” under the VIC club membership fees).

During the role-play performance, feel free to ask any of the following questions or additional questions:
Why should I have to pay the service call fee after such a short amount of time after my annual check-up?
How come the service technician didn’t do a cycle check to test the furnace after the first turn on?
How long will I have to wait for somebody to come to the house?  It is getting cold.

The customer service rep should open the presentation by pretending to answer the phone.

There is no right or wrong answer to the event. However, the participant’s ability to defend his/her policy and position based on the facts provided (and those that you care to add) should be the basis for the score. The plan should be presented with clarity and conviction.

Feel free to enhance the story as much as you want, but if you enhance it for one individual, make sure you enhance it for all the individuals.



# FBLA Client Service Final National

2009 SLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
FINAL CASE STUDY

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS

You have 10 minutes to review the case and may use the provided note cards in the presentation. Note cards will be collected upon leaving
The presentation is interactive with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentation.
Presentation time is 5 minutes. At 4 and 5 minutes the timekeeper will stand.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS

A logical solution is selected and presented with positive and negative aspects of its implementation given
Alternatives are recognized with pros and cons stated and evaluated
Translate case into effective, efficient, and spontaneous action
Possess good decision-making and problem-solving skills
Thoughts and statements are well organized and clearly stated; appropriate business language is used
Demonstrates ability to effectively communicate with client.

CASE STUDY SITUATION

You are to assume the role of Client Service Intern in the Customer Relations Division of the Mountain Lodge and Inn Spa and Resort. Your job is to handle problems with the help of the Division Manager if needed when customers are not pleased with their lodging, entertainment, food service, or spa treatments. Your Inn is known for its fabulous facilities, super service with courteous and highly professional employees. Rarely do you ever have a serious complaint. But today is turning out to be a red-letter day for sure!

You have a lady standing at your desk mad and wet. She is red all over and is ready to dump on you or anyone she can. You recognize her as one of your frequent visitors and know that she stays with you, takes part in all activities, and is generous with praise and tips for good service. Obviously, she is not ready for either one of those right now.

You greet her cordially by smiling and saying, “Hello, Ms. Lindenauer, how may I make your day special?” Wrong words! She replies, “It is already special, thank you! I was eating my lunch or preparing to, that is. I specifically told the server who apparently is new that I wanted the seafood special with the veggies but—and here is where things really went wrong—I am very allergic to even the smell of bell peppers and to be sure that none were cooked in or near my veggies. My server took my order and off she went.
Promptly, as usual, my order was brought to me. When she set the lovely dish down, I was stunned by the sight and smell of bell peppers—red ones, green ones, and yellow ones all over the veggies—it seemed they were just glaring at me! I lost my cool right then and told my server to get that dastardly plate away from me and to put my order in correctly and bring it back without those bell peppers! She started making excuses for the kitchen, and I wanted no part of it. And then and then—she picked up the plate, off slid some of the veggies right on me! She then grabbed a towel and a glass of water and started getting the mess off me—which made it even worse. So here I am, a ruined lunch and ruined clothes; I am already getting sick from the bell peppers as I am highly allergic to them as I told my server! Now, what are you going to do?”

You know you have a very serious situation here with this frequent customer. You know she is very allergic to bell peppers and her face is already getting red spots (not to blend in with the red from being so angry) on her face. You know that you have a great medical team on duty in the Spa and that your in-house laundry can take care of her ruined clothing. But what are you going to do now? Ms. Lindenauer is standing with her hands on her ruined clothes, pulling them away from her body. And she is wet from the botched clean up work of the server.

Once you are ready for your presentation, the manager (the judge) will act as the lady with the problem. The manager (judge) will greet you and then will follow your lead in talking about the care needed immediately for this customer. The manager (judge) will then critique your presentation briefly, asking questions in the three-minute question-and-answer session.

Once you make your presentation, the manager (judge) will conclude the role-play activity by thanking you for your presentation. You will surrender your two note cards at this time.

FBLA CLIENT SERVICE FINAL
JUDGE NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION

This is a role-playing event.
Review the “Judges Instructions” and the case.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Each of the members of the team should respond to at least one question or issue. The time allowed is 5 minutes.
After asking the standard questions below, you may ask other questions specific to the current participant. However, if you do this for one team, you must ask additional questions to all the teams.
You will close the event by thanking the participants for their input.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES INSTRUCTIONS

In this Client Service Intern performance, you will assume the role of the customer who approaches the Customer Relations desk with an extreme problem.

Ms. Lindenauer is a long-time frequent guest at your facility. She was at lunch, specifically told her server that she is very allergic to any kind of bell peppers. She asked the server to be sure her veggies were not cooked with or even near any peppers, not red, green, or yellow ones. When the server brought her lunch, there were bell peppers everywhere. You lost it right then and asked the server to take it away immediately and bring back a correct order. The server started making excuses for the kitchen as she picked up the dish, letting the veggies and those peppers fall all over you. Then the server gets the glass of water, pours some on a towel and on you and starts mopping up your clothes. You are furious. You demand a resolution right now.

The participant (Client Service Intern) will make the performance presentation to you, as you play the role of the customer.

During the role-play performance, ask the following questions of each participant:
Why didn’t the server listen and write down the order and the allergy information?
What can the laundry do to make my clothes right again?
What about my face and this allergy? I could die right here if this rash spreads. What are you going to do?
What steps will or has your office taken to assure that the servers know how to react when a customer mentions an allergic condition?
Could you have greeted the guest differently when you saw her standing at the desk with food all over her, red in the face, and wet?
Once the Client Service Intern (participant) has made his or her presentation and answered your questions, you will conclude the role-play situation by stating that you appreciate the suggestions.

2009 SLC FBLA CLIENT SERVICE
PRELIMINARY CASE STUDY

PARTICIPANT INSTRUCTIONS

You have 10 minutes to review the case and may use the two provided note cards in the presentation. Note cards will be collected upon leaving
The presentation is interactive with the judges who will ask questions throughout the presentation.
Presentation time is 5 minutes. At 4 and 5 minutes the timekeeper will stand.

PERFORMANCE INDICATORS

A logical solution is selected and presented with positive and negative aspects of its implementation given
Alternatives are recognized with pros and cons stated and evaluated
Translate case into effective, efficient, and spontaneous action
Possess good decision-making and problem-solving skills
Thoughts and statements are well organized and clearly stated; appropriate business language is used
Demonstrates ability to effectively communicate with client.

CASE STUDY SITUATION

You are to assume the role of Client Service Intern for the largest newspaper in the area. You work in the Customer Relations Division and contact previous customers who no longer subscribe to The Daily Express. Your job is to get them back as current subscribers. Your calling schedule is pretty busy because a lot of customers dropped their subscriptions when the delivery service became a bit lax. Customers’ papers were thrown in the bushes, on top of cars in the drive, or not delivered at all. When it rained, the customers found their paper wet and muddy on most rainy days. And if it snowed, wait for the thaw to retrieve your newspaper was the word going around town!

You have just called your 10th former customer and that customer is letting you have a piece of his or her mind. You are shocked at what you are hearing and do not know what to say. You know from your brief training for the job that you cannot yell at the customer nor be rude in any way because word will spread and subscriptions will fall even more.

You know you have a very unhappy former customer here who wants to have a good newspaper delivered in the right way at a good price. How will you handle this situation? What procedure will you follow to make him or her happy so that you will get a former subscriber back on your list of happy customers? You know the newspaper is rated very highly in advertising
and in feature interests. You know the price is competitive with other local city newspapers. What you need to convince this customer is that your delivery service is now back to what it should be. Customers are getting their papers right at the front door and are expressing their satisfaction with the new manager of the team. You have asked your manager (the judge) to give you some feedback on your response to unhappy and/or angry customers that you call. The manager (judge) will meet you in a few minutes in his or her office. Give the performance of your life here to help the customer get back onboard your subscription list.

Once you are ready for your presentation, the manager (the judge) will act as an irate customer. The manager (judge) will greet you and then will follow your lead in talking about the renewal of the newspaper subscription for this customer. The manager (judge) will then critique your presentation briefly, asking questions in the three-minute question-and-answer session.

Once you make your presentation, the manager (judge) will conclude the role-play activity by thanking you for your presentation. You will surrender your two note cards at this time.

FBLA CLIENT SERVICE PRELIMINARY
JUDGE NOTES

JUDGING THE PRESENTATION

This is a role-playing event.
Review the “Judges Instructions” and the case.
This is an interactive problem, so treat the presentation as a conversation. Each of the members of the team should respond to at least one question or issue. The time allowed is 5 minutes.
After asking the standard questions below, you may ask other questions specific to the current participant. However, if you do this for one team, you must ask additional questions to all the teams.
You will close the event by thanking the participants for their input.
Complete the rating sheet.

JUDGES INSTRUCTIONS

In this Client Service Intern performance, you will assume the role of the irate former customer on the telephone.

The customer has cancelled his or her subscription to the newspaper because he or she was just fed up with paying for a paper that was either left in the bushes, on top of the car in the drive, in a wet puddle, or even knee deep in snow, for gosh sakes. He or she complained many times about the poor delivery service to no avail—it never got better!!

The participant (Client Service Intern) will make the performance presentation to you, as you play the role of the customer.

During the role-play performance, ask the following questions of each participant:

Why did your manager let this situation go by for so long? I know most of my friends also cancelled. We would rather buy the paper at the stand, get a cup of coffee, and enjoy the paper peacefully and cleanly. Why should I subscribe and get a mess again?
How can you assure me that the service has improved?
What kind of training did the delivery team get to have them back on track with prompt and efficient delivery?
What steps will or has your office taken to assure me this will not happen again?

Once the Client Service Intern (participant) has made his or her presentation and answered your questions, you will conclude the role-play situation by stating that you appreciate the suggestions.
